request.question: "Ricardio the Heart Guy" is an episode of an animated television series that was created by who?

title: Business Guy
Clown in the Dumps
Family Guy: Back to the Multiverse
Cartoon Wars Part II
The Simpsons Guy
Ricardio the Heart Guy
Adventure Time (season 1)
Lady & Peebles
Brian & Stewie
The Itchy & Scratchy & Poochie Show
sentences: "Business Guy" is the ninth episode in the eighth season of the American animated television series "Family Guy".
It originally aired on Fox in the United States on December 13, 2009.
The episode centers on Peter as he assumes temporary control of his father-in-law Carter Pewterschmidt's billion-dollar empire after he suffers a heart attack and is incapacitated in a coma.

Peter immediately becomes power hungry, however, and develops several ridiculous items, prompting Lois and Carter, after he recovers from his coma, to try to trick him into relinquishing control of the company.
"Clown in the Dumps" is the season premiere of the twenty-sixth season of the American animated television series "The Simpsons", and the 553rd episode of the series overall.
It first aired in the United States on the Fox network on September 28, 2014, with "The Simpsons Guy", a crossover episode of "Family Guy" with "The Simpsons", airing afterwards.
This episode was dedicated in memory of Louis Castellaneta, the father of "The Simpsons" voice actor Dan Castellaneta.
It was written by Joel H. Cohen and directed by Steven Dean Moore, with Don Hertzfeldt directing a sequence in the opening titles.

Jeff Ross, Sarah Silverman and David Hyde Pierce guest starred as themselves, with Jackie Mason and Kelsey Grammer reprising their respective roles as Rabbi Krustofski and Sideshow Bob, while Maurice LaMarche voiced several minor characters.

Family Guy: Back to the Multiverse is an action-adventure game that was released for Microsoft Windows, PlayStation 3 and Xbox 360 on November 20, 2012, in North America, November 21, 2012, in Australia and November 23, 2012, in Europe.
The game is based on the American animated television series "Family Guy", most notably the episode "Road to the Multiverse", and is also a continuation of the episode "The Big Bang Theory".
This game also features the return of Stewie's evil half-brother Bertram, who was killed in the show.
"Back to the Multiverse" is the first "Family Guy" console game since "Family Guy Video Game!

When the game was available for pre-order, people who pre-ordered the game received a special level, based on "", another video game based on the "Aliens" trademark also owned by 20th Century Fox, which was released the next February to similar negative reception.
"Cartoon Wars Part II" is the fourth episode in the tenth season of the American animated television series "South Park".
The 143rd episode of the series overall, it first aired on Comedy Central in the United States on April 12, 2006.

After "Cartoon Wars Part I", it is the second part of a two-episode story-arc, which focuses on Cartman's efforts to get the television series "Family Guy" cancelled, by exploiting fears of retaliation by Muslims to an impending "Family Guy" episode in which the Islamic prophet Muhammad will appear, in violation of some interpretations of Muslim law.
Kyle instead urges the president of the network airing "Family Guy", Fox, to air the episode in an exercise of free speech.
"The Simpsons Guy" is the first episode of the thirteenth season of the American animated television series "Family Guy", and the 232nd overall episode.
"The Simpsons Guy" is a 45-minute-long crossover with "The Simpsons", and was written by Patrick Meighan and directed by Peter Shin.
It originally aired in the United States on September 28, 2014, on Fox, where both "The Simpsons" and "Family Guy" have aired since their respective debuts.
"Ricardio the Heart Guy" is the seventh episode of the first season of the American animated television series "Adventure Time".
The episode was written and storyboarded by Bert Youn and Sean Jimenez, from a story by Merriwether Williams, Tim McKeon, and Adam Muto.
It originally aired on Cartoon Network on April 26, 2010.
The episode guest stars George Takei as the title character, Ricardio.
The first season of "Adventure Time", an American animated television series created by Pendleton Ward, premiered on Cartoon Network on April 5, 2010 and concluded on September 27, 2010.
The season was produced by Cartoon Network Studios and Frederator Studios.
The series is based on a short produced for Frederator's Nicktoons Network animation incubator series "Random!
Cartoons".
The season follows the adventures of Finn, a human boy, and his best friend and adoptive brother Jake, a dog with magical powers to change shape and size at will.

Finn and Jake live in the post-apocalyptic Land of Ooo, where they interact with the other main characters of the show: Princess Bubblegum, The Ice King, Marceline the Vampire Queen, Lumpy Space Princess, and BMO.
"Lady & Peebles" is the nineteenth episode of the fourth season of the American animated television series "Adventure Time".
The episode was written and storyboarded by Cole Sanchez and Rebecca Sugar, from a story by Patrick McHale, Kent Osborne, and Pendleton Ward.
It originally aired on Cartoon Network on August 20, 2012.
The episode guest stars George Takei as Ricardio.
"Brian & Stewie" is the 17th episode of the eighth season of the American animated television series "Family Guy", and 150th episode of the series overall.
It originally aired on Fox in the United States on May 2, 2010.
The episode features anthropomorphic dog Brian and baby Stewie after they are accidentally trapped inside a bank vault over a weekend.
The two are ultimately forced to reveal their true feelings about each other, and eventually go on to question each other's existence and purpose in life.
Brian and Stewie become even closer to each other as time goes on, and climactically help each other survive being trapped inside the vault.

The bottle episode breaks from the show's usual set-up, and is the only episode of the series not to feature any music or use any cutaway gags with Brian and Stewie being the only two characters featured in the entire episode.
In repeats of the episode there is no main title sequence, nor is any music played over the end credits.
"The Itchy & Scratchy & Poochie Show" is the fourteenth episode in the eighth season of the American animated television series "The Simpsons".
It originally aired on the Fox network in the United States on February 9, 1997.
In the episode, "The Itchy & Scratchy Show" attempts to regain viewers by introducing a new character named Poochie, whose voice is provided by Homer.
The episode is largely self-referential and satirizes the world of television production, fans of "The Simpsons", and the series itself.
It was written by David X. Cohen and directed by Steven Dean Moore.

Alex Rocco is a credited guest voice as Roger Meyers, Jr. for the third and final time (having previously provided the character's voice in "Itchy & Scratchy & Marge" and "The Day the Violence Died"); Phil Hartman also guest stars as Troy McClure.
Poochie would become a minor recurring character and Comic Book Guy's catchphrase, "Worst episode ever", is introduced in this episode.
With "The Itchy & Scratchy & Poochie Show", the show's 167th episode, "The Simpsons" surpassed "The Flintstones" in the number of episodes produced for a prime-time animated series.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Additional constraints (must follow):
- The first sentence must be an answer-first concise identification (a direct answer or concise identification). If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the inputs (context or passages) and paraphrase no more than one or two simple attributes.
- When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection is mandatory and must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to the extractive rules below. Use the extractor that matches the question intent and only apply an extractor when the summaries present the required adjacent or verbatim phrasing.

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind").
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

The episode was written and storyboarded by Bert Youn and Sean Jimenez, from a story by Merriwether Williams, Tim McKeon, and Adam Muto.  It originally aired on Cartoon Network on April 26, 2010.  The episode guest stars George Takei as the title character, Ricardio.

Lady & Peebles | "Lady & Peebles" is the nineteenth episode of the fourth season of the American animated television series "Adventure Time".  The episode was written and storyboarded by Cole Sanchez and Rebecca Sugar, from a story by Patrick McHale, Kent Osborne, and Pendleton Ward.  It originally aired on Cartoon Network on August 20, 2012.  The episode guest stars George Takei as Ricardio.

SantApprentice | SantApprentice is an animated television series in 50 episodes of 12 minutes and two 26 minute episodes created by Jan Van Rijsselberge and made by Alphanim (now Gaumont Animation) in 2006.  The holiday series follows the adventures of Nicolas, a young orphan boy from Sydney, Australia, who is a pure of heart and believes in Santa Claus as he is Santa's Apprentice.  Nicolas's job is to achieve his tasks to become full-fledged Santa.

List of Family Guy writers | The following is a list of writers who have worked on the Fox animated television series "Family Guy" in the order of first credited episode (by broadcast).  As of 7 December 2014, 60 people have been credited with writing or co-writing at least one episode of "Family Guy".

You Can't Do That on Television, Peter | "You Can't Do That on Television, Peter" is the eighteenth episode of the tenth season of the animated comedy series, "Family Guy".  The episode originally aired on Fox in the United States on April 1, 2012.  In the episode, Peter creates and stars in a children's TV show after his favourite show is cancelled.  Meanwhile, Meg shadows Dr. Hartman at the Quahog hospital.

Business Guy | "Business Guy" is the ninth episode in the eighth season of the American animated television series "Family Guy".  It originally aired on Fox in the United States on December 13, 2009.  The episode centers on Peter as he assumes temporary control of his father-in-law Carter Pewterschmidt's billion-dollar empire after he suffers a heart attack and is incapacitated in a coma.  Peter immediately becomes power hungry, however, and develops several ridiculous items, prompting Lois and Carter, after he recovers from his coma, to try to trick him into relinquishing control of the company.

Steve Marmel | Steve Marmel (born December 17, 1964) is an American television writer and producer who has worked on many animated TV series, including "The Fairly OddParents", "I Am Weasel", "Danny Phantom", "Family Guy" and "Yin Yang Yo! ".  During his work on "The Fairly OddParents" he frequently co-wrote episodes with Butch Hartman.  Marmel also created the series "Sonny with a Chance" as well as the series "Mech-X4".

response.summary_1:
Primary topic: "Ricardio the Heart Guy" — Adventure Time episode.

- "'Ricardio the Heart Guy' is the seventh episode of the first season" of the American animated series "Adventure Time."  
- "Originally aired on Cartoon Network on April 26, 2010."  
- "Written and storyboarded by Bert Youn and Sean Jimenez," with story by "Merriwether Williams, Tim McKeon, and Adam Muto."  
- "The episode guest stars George Takei as the title character, Ricardio."  
- Passage [2] lists Pendleton Ward as one of the story contributors for another Adventure Time episode ("Lady & Peebles" — "from a story by ... Pendleton Ward").

Missing / ambiguous: The passages do NOT state who "created" the Adventure Time series (the required "created by" fact is missing). Candidates mentioned in the passages (ambiguous with respect to "creator"): Pendleton Ward (appears as story contributor), Adam Muto, Merriwether Williams, Tim McKeon.

Next-hop retrieval (priority order):
1. Fetch the "Adventure Time" (TV series) page — inspect lede/infobox "Created by" and "Development/Production" sections.  
2. Fetch "Pendleton Ward" page — inspect lede/infobox and "Career" for a creator credit.  
3. Search keywords if needed: "Adventure Time created by" or "who created Adventure Time" (target authoritative sources/Wikipedia).
hop2_docs: Mia's Math Adventure: Just in Time! | Mia's Math Adventure: Just in Time!  is the third title of the "Mia's Big Adventure Collection" software series created by Kutoka Interactive.  Released in 2001 in Canada and the United States, the game teaches mathematics to children between 6 and 10 years old.

Time Gentlemen, Please! (video game) | Time Gentlemen, Please!  is a point-and-click adventure game developed by Zombie Cow Studios.  It is the sequel to the freeware game "Ben There, Dan That! " and was commercially released on June 22, 2009.  As with the first game, "Time Gentlemen, Please!"  was created using the Adventure Game Studio engine.

Adventure Time (pilot) | "Adventure Time" is an animated short created by Pendleton Ward, as well as the pilot to the Cartoon Network series of the same name.  The short follows the adventures of Pen (voiced by Zack Shada), a human boy, and his best friend Jake (voiced by John DiMaggio), a dog with magical powers to change shape and grow and shrink at will.  In this episode, Pen and Jake have to rescue Princess Bubblegum (voiced by Paige Moss) from the antagonistic Ice King (voiced by John Kassir).

The Labyrinth of Time | The Labyrinth of Time is a graphic adventure computer game created by Terra Nova Development, a two-man team composed of Bradley W. Schenck and Michal Todorovic.  Intended to be the first in a series of games, "The Labyrinth of Time" was less successful than similar graphic adventures released around the same time, such as "The 7th Guest" and "Myst".  It is the sole game produced by Terra Nova Development.  In the years after its release, "The Labyrinth of Time" was published on more recent platforms by The Wyrmkeep Entertainment Co. in collaboration with the original developers.

Future-Worm! | Future-Worm!  is an American comic science fiction animated television series created by Ryan Quincy, who previously created IFC's "Out There", for Disney XD.  The show features the adventures of Danny and Future-Worm, who time travel by using a lunch box-shaped time machine.

Ark of Time | Ark of Time is a 1997 adventure game.  Originally created and distributed across Europe, it was later published by Koei Corporation and developed by Trecision S.p.A. in the United States.

The Sarah Jane Adventures | The Sarah Jane Adventures is a British science fiction television programme, that was produced by BBC Cymru Wales for CBBC, created by Russell T Davies starring Elisabeth Sladen.  The programme is a spin-off of the long-running BBC science fiction programme "Doctor Who" and is aimed at a younger audience than "Doctor Who".  It focuses on the adventures of Sarah Jane Smith, an investigative journalist who, as a young woman, had numerous adventures across time and space.
hop2_titles: Mia's Math Adventure: Just in Time!
response.summary_2: Pendleton Ward created the animated television series Adventure Time. Pendleton Ward created the animated short "Adventure Time," which served as the pilot for the Cartoon Network series.
